content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

Top Take-Home Messages.1: Disease stages in patients with valvular heart disease should be classified based on symptoms, valve anatomy, severity of valve dysfunction, and ventricular/pulmonary circulation response.
Top Take-Home Messages.2: Evaluation of patients should include correlation of history and physical examination with noninvasive testing to determine treatment strategy.
Top Take-Home Messages.3: Decision on oral anticoagulation should be made collaboratively for patients with valvular heart disease and atrial fibrillation.
Top Take-Home Messages.4: Consideration for valve intervention is advised for all patients with severe valvular heart disease, with multidisciplinary team evaluation.
Top Take-Home Messages.5: Treatment of severe aortic stenosis should be symptom-driven, considering exercise testing and biomarkers.
Top Take-Home Messages.6: Indications for transcatheter aortic valve implantation are expanding, with decision-making involving consideration of lifetime risks and benefits.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.Source: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
content.Overview: This guideline provides recommendations for the management of valvular heart diseases, focusing on improving cardiovascular health through scientific and evidence-based guidelines.
content.Clinical Implementation: Management is effective only when recommendations are followed by both practitioners and patients, facilitated by shared decision-making.

Indications for Treatment.Detail: Intervention is advisable for relief of symptoms and prevention of left ventricular volume overload.
Mitral transcatheter edge-to-edge repair can benefit patients at high risk for surgery.

content.Bioprosthetic Valve Dysfunction: This may occur due to leaflet degeneration or thrombosis, and catheter-based treatment is reasonable in selected patients.

content.title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.subject: Guideline for management of valvular heart disease
key_points: The guideline includes recommendations for guideline-directed management and therapy (GDMT) encompassing clinical evaluation, diagnostic testing, and both pharmacological and procedural treatments.
The guideline is based on evidence from randomized controlled trials, observational studies, systematic reviews, and expert opinion.
An independent evidence review committee supports guideline development by ensuring a thorough review of existing evidence.

introduction.methodology: The recommendations are based on extensive review of literature published through March 1, 2020, focusing on valvular heart disease, associated conditions, and surgical interventions.
introduction.writing_committee_organization: Composed of cardiologists, interventionalists, surgeons, among others to ensure diverse expertise.

Review and Approval.Process: The document was reviewed by official reviewers from ACC and AHA, as well as content reviewers.

Scope.Focus: The guideline focuses on diagnosis and management of adult patients with valvular heart disease (VHD).
Scope.Updates: Full revisions were made in 2008 and 2014, with updates in 2017 and special clarification on surgery for aortic dilation in patients with bicuspid aortic valves (BAV) in 2016.

content.Purpose: To provide concise, evidence-based recommendations and supporting documentation for clinicians managing patients with VHD.

Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 1: Strong recommendation; benefit >>> risk. Suggested phrases include 'is recommended' and 'should be performed'.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 2a: Moderate recommendation; benefit >> risk. Suggested phrases include 'is reasonable' and 'can be useful'.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 2b: Weak recommendation; benefit ≥ risk. Suggested phrases include 'may/might be reasonable' and 'usefulness is unclear'.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 3: No benefit; benefit = risk. Suggested phrases include 'is not recommended' and 'should not be used'.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 3 Harm: Strong harm; risk > benefit. Suggested phrases include 'potentially harmful' and 'causes harm'.

Level of Evidence (LOE).Level A: High-quality evidence from multiple RCTs or meta-analyses.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level B-R: Moderate-quality evidence from one or more RCTs.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level C-LD: Limited data from randomized or nonrandomized studies.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level C-EO: Expert opinion based on clinical experience.

content.Notes: COR and LOE are evaluated independently. A low LOE does not imply a weak recommendation.

general_principles.evaluation_of_patient: Patients with known or suspected valvular heart disease (VHD) should undergo an initial meticulous history and physical examination. Additional diagnostic tests include ECG, chest x-ray, TTE, and ancillary tests such as TEE, CT, CMR, stress testing, Holter monitoring, diagnostic hemodynamic cardiac catheterization, or PET combined with CT imaging.
general_principles.definitions_of_severity: Valve disease severity is classified based on symptoms, valve anatomy, valve hemodynamics, and the effects of valve dysfunction on ventricular and vascular function. Indications for intervention and monitoring depend on symptoms, severity of VHD, the response of the LV, volume or pressure overload caused by VHD, and effects on pulmonary or systemic circulation.

Patients with progressive VHD (mild to moderate severity and asymptomatic)

Asymptomatic patients who have the criteria for severe VHD. 1: Asymptomatic patients with severe VHD in whom the LV or RV remains compensated, 2: Asymptomatic patients with severe VHD with decompensation of the LV or RV

Initial Diagnosis.TTE: It is the standard diagnostic test in the initial evaluation of patients with known or suspected VHD. TTE allows accurate assessment of valve anatomy and etiology, concurrent valve disease, and associated abnormalities such as aortic dilation and LV anatomy and function.
Initial Diagnosis.Doppler Echocardiography: Provides accurate noninvasive determination of valve hemodynamics.

Follow-Up.Routine Follow-Up: After initial evaluation, clinicians should continue regular follow-up with periodic exams and TTE to prevent irreversible consequences of severe VHD.
Follow-Up.Frequency of Testing: Depends on the type and severity of the valve lesion, the rate of progression, and the effect on the function of the valve.

Diagnosis and Follow-Up.Cardiac Catheterization: Necessary in some patients to ensure proper treatment decisions when noninvasive tests are inconclusive.

content.Guideline Summary: The guideline focuses on the management of valvular heart disease (VHD). A hemodynamic cardiac catheterization may be used when noninvasive tests are ambiguous. Contrast angiography can be used for semiquantitative assessment of valve regurgitation when noninvasive results are discordant with physical examination. Diagnostic interventions include exercise hemodynamics, and testing with low-flow states.
content.Exercise Testing: Exercise stress testing helps determine the optimal timing for potential intervention and identifies symptomatic patients.
content.Basic Principles of Medical Therapy: Patients with VHD should follow heart-healthy lifestyle factors. Exercise and standard GDMT for cardiac risk factors should not be neglected. Intervention is indicated for patients with LV systolic dysfunction and severe VHD.
content.Secondary Prevention of Rheumatic Fever: Secondary prevention is recommended for patients with rheumatic heart disease. Long-term antistreptococcal prophylaxis is indicated.
content.Recommendations: Patients with VHD should maintain a healthy lifestyle, undergo appropriate testing and receive necessary prophylactic treatment.

Antibiotics for Prevention.Macrolide or azalide antibiotic: Varies (for patients allergic to penicillin and sulfadiazine)

Duration of Secondary Prophylaxis for Rh.Rheumatic fever with carditis and residu: 10 years or until patient is 40 years old
Duration of Secondary Prophylaxis for Rh.Rheumatic fever with carditis but no res: 10 years or until patient is 21 years old
Duration of Secondary Prophylaxis for Rh.Rheumatic fever without carditis: 5 years or until patient is 21 years old

Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended: Prosthetic cardiac valves or transcatheter-implanted prostheses
Prosthetic material used for cardiac valve repair
Unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease with residual defects
Antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommende: In patients with VHD at high risk of IE for routine procedures like TEE, esophagogastroduodenoscopy, colonoscopy, or cystoscopy

Evidence and Studies.RCTs indicate limited data on antibiotic: Prospective study showed reduced risk in high-risk dental patients but no elimination of bacteremia
Evidence and Studies.Cochrane Database 2013: No strong evidence for antibiotic prophylaxis in dentistry

Highest Risk Factors: Congenital heart disease with residual flow disturbances

Prophylaxis.Postoperative Measures: Antimicrobial therapy for patients with bacteriuria

Recommendations.Recommendation: For patients with AF and native valve heart disease or bioprosthetic valve >3 months, use NOAC as an alternative to VKA
For patients with AF and rheumatic MS, long-term VKA oral anticoagulation is recommended
For patients with new-onset AF ≤3 months, anticoagulation with a VKA is reasonable
For patients with mechanical heart valves, NOACs are not recommended

Synopsis.VKA: Anticoagulant for rheumatic MS and mechanical heart valves
Synopsis.NOAC: Alternative to VKA for native VHD excluding rheumatic MS

content.Overview: The guideline addresses anticoagulation in patients with valvular heart disease (VHD) and atrial fibrillation (AF). It distinguishes between patients with different types of valve disease and post-procedural timelines for managing anticoagulation therapy.
Key Points: Patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis (MS) require long-term VKA anticoagulation.
For native valve disease (except rheumatic MS) and bioprosthetic valves >3 months after procedure, anticoagulation is based on the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score using NOACs or VKA.
For new-onset AF within 3 months of bioprosthetic valve implantation, VKA is recommended.
Moderate to severe rheumatic MS and mechanical heart valves are unique cases that require different considerations.
content.NOAC vs VKA: No differential effect has been observed between NOAC and VKA therapy, but fewer thromboembolic events have been reported with NOACs.
Additional Insights: Postoperative AF after VHD intervention is associated with increased stroke and mortality rates.
There is ongoing evaluation and conflicting data about the safety of NOACs in specific settings, like after bioprosthesis implantation.

content.Synopsis: Risk assessment is pivotal in evaluating patients with valvular heart disease (VHD) for whom intervention may be required. Scoring systems, though limited, are useful in determining procedural risks and benefits. The availability of TAVI for aortic stenosis has widened the surgical risk spectrum.

Recommendation.Text: For patients with VHD considering intervention, calculate individual risks for surgical or transcatheter procedures using online tools, incorporating these into a shared decision-making process.

Key Points: Discuss risks like age at implantation, valve durability, and pacemaker needs, especially for younger patients (<65 years). Sequential procedural strategies must be considered (e.g., valve-in-valve procedures).
The decision to intervene and type of procedure should be based on patient, procedure, and institution-specific risks and benefits.

Criteria: Cardiac or other organ system compromise

Risk Categories.Details: <3%, None frailty, No organ system compromise, No procedure-specific impediments.
<1%, None frailty, No organ system compromise, No procedure-specific impediments.
>8% mortality or morbidity, ≤1 frailty index, 1-2 organ systems compromised, Possible impediment.
Predicted mortality/morbidity >50%, ≥2 frailty indices, ≥3 organ systems compromised, Severe impediment.

content.Title: Recommendations for the Multidisciplinary Heart Valve Team and Heart Valve Centers

Recommendations.Recommendation: Patients with severe VHD should be evaluated by a Multidisciplinary Heart Valve Team (MDT) when intervention is considered.

Consultation with or referral to a Primary or Comprehensive Heart Valve Center is reasonable when treatment options are being discussed for asymptomatic patients with severe VHD, patients who may benefit from valve repair versus valve replacement, or patients with multiple comorbidities for whom valve intervention is considered.

content.Synopsis: The MDT is an established feature of heart valve programs and has been formally endorsed by the ACC, the American Society of Echocardiography, and the Society.
content.Geral Information: The MDT has become increasingly important in the treatment of Valvular Heart Disease (VHD) as it allows for optimized patient outcomes through improved decision-making and matching of patients to appropriate services.

content.Management: Interventions in patients with valvular heart disease (VHD) include both transcatheter and surgical approaches. Regular evaluation post-intervention is necessary for monitoring and managing complications. Endocarditis prophylaxis and antithrombotic therapy for prosthetic valves are important aspects of care.
content.Procedural Complications: Postoperative atrial fibrillation is a common issue occurring in up to one-third of patients. Complications can include stroke, heart block, heart failure, and infections, particularly after surgical interventions like AVR.
content.Primary and Secondary Treatment: Coronary artery disease (CAD) is often concurrent with VHD. Treatment involves risk factors management consistent with current guidelines for CAD.
content.Persistent Symptoms: Post-intervention symptoms should be carefully assessed to rule out restenosis, regurgitation, or valve complications. Evaluation and treatment of cardiac and noncardiac conditions are crucial.
content.Periodic Imaging: Regular imaging with transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is recommended post-intervention. The timing and frequency depend on the type of intervention and patient symptoms.

imaging_follow_up.bioprosthetic_valve_surgery: Baseline, 5 and 10 years after surgery, then annually
imaging_follow_up.mitral_valve_repair_surgery: Baseline, 1 year, then every 2 to 3 years
imaging_follow_up.bicuspid_aortic_valve_disease: Continued post-AVR monitoring if aortic size is ≥4.0 cm at time of AVR

imaging_notes.additional_imaging: CT, fluoroscopy CMR, or PET for concern of valve dysfunction or endocarditis

aortic_stenosis.management: Depends on accurate diagnosis of cause and stage

stage_a.hemodynamics: Aortic Vmax <2 m/s with normal leaflet motion

valve_anatomy: Mild to moderate leaflet calcification/fibrosis of a bicuspid or trileaflet valve
Rheumatic valve changes with commissural fusion

hemodynamics.mild_as: Aortic Vmax 2.0–2.9 m/s or mean ΔP <20 mm Hg
hemodynamics.moderate_as: Aortic Vmax 3.0–3.9 m/s or mean ΔP 20–39 mm Hg

stage_b.hemodynamic_consequences: Early LV diastolic dysfunction may be present; Normal LVEF

stage_c1.valve_anatomy: Severe leaflet calcification/fibrosis or congenital stenosis with severely reduced leaflet opening

hemodynamics.aortic_vmax: ≥4 m/s or mean ΔP ≥40 mm Hg
hemodynamics.very_severe_as: Aortic Vmax ≥5 m/s or mean P ≥60 mm Hg

stage_c1.hemodynamic_consequences: LV diastolic dysfunction or Mild LV hypertrophy; Normal LVEF
stage_c1.symptoms: None; Exercise testing is reasonable to confirm symptom status

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

Stage Definitions.Definition: Asymptomatic severe AS with LV ejection fraction <50%
Stage Definitions.Valve Anatomy: Severe leaflet calcification/fibrosis or congenital stenosis with severely reduced leaflet opening
Stage Definitions.Valve Hemodynamics: Aortic Vmax ≥4 m/s or mean ΔP ≥40 mm Hg; AVA typically ≤1.0 cm²

Stage Definitions.Valve Anatomy: Severe leaflet calcification/fibrosis or congenital stenosis with severely reduced leaflet opening
Stage Definitions.Valve Hemodynamics: Aortic Vmax ≥4 m/s or mean ΔP ≥40 mm Hg; AVA typically ≤1.0 cm²

Stage Definitions.Definition: Symptomatic severe low-flow, low-gradient AS with reduced LVEF
Stage Definitions.Valve Anatomy: Severe leaflet calcification/fibrosis with severely reduced leaflet motion
Valve Hemodynamics: AVA ≤1.0 cm² with resting aortic Vmax <4 m/s or mean ΔP <40 mm Hg
Dobutamine stress echocardiography shows AVA <1.0 cm² with Vmax ≥4 m/s

Stage Definitions.Definition: Symptomatic severe low-gradient/severe AS with normal LVEF or paradoxical low-flow severe AS
Stage Definitions.Valve Anatomy: Severe leaflet calcification/fibrosis with severely reduced leaflet motion
Valve Hemodynamics: AVA ≤1.0 cm² with aortic Vmax <4 m/s or mean ΔP <40 mm Hg
Measured with patient is normotensive (systolic blood pressure <140 mm Hg)

Recommendations for Diagnostic Testing.Recommendation: TTE is indicated for accurate diagnosis of the cause of AS, assessment of hemodynamic severity, measurement of LV size and systolic function, and determination of prognosis and timing of valve intervention.

In patients with suspected low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with normal LVEF (Stage D3), optimization of blood pressure control is recommended before measurement of AS severity by TTE, TEI, cardiac catheterization, or CMR.

content.Summary: The guideline addresses the management of valvular heart disease (VHD), particularly in patients with aortic stenosis (AS). It outlines the initial diagnosis approach and condition-specific recommendations, emphasizing the use of echocardiographic imaging and other diagnostic measurements in assessing the severity of AS.

Recommendations.Recommendation: For patients with suspected low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), low-dose dobutamine stress testing is recommended to evaluate valve severity and contractile reserve.
For patients with suspected low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with normal or reduced LVEF, calculating the outflow tract to aortic velocity ratio is advised to assess severity.
Measurement of aortic valve calcium score via CT imaging is recommended for patients with suspected low-flow, low-gradient severe AS to define severity.

Details.Point: Aortic valve area, transaortic velocity, and other measurements are crucial for diagnosis and decision-making in AS. Systemic hypertension can influence diagnostic readings, necessitating repeated measurements under normotensive conditions.
Severe AS is characterized by a fixed valve area. An increase in transaortic velocity at a specific threshold is indicative of severity.
Aortic valve calcification is a predictor of clinical outcome, with certain thresholds indicating severity.

repeat_TTE.indications: In patients with known valvular AS and changing signs or symptoms. Also recommended in patients with AS with increased hemodynamic demands.
repeat_TTE.clinical_settings: Noncardiac surgery, pregnancy, infection, anemia, gastrointestinal bleeding.

routine_follow_up.timing: Depends on comorbidities, patient-specific factors, and AS severity.

symptom_progression.severe_AS: Annual event-free survival rate of 30% to 50% at 2 years.

moderate_AS.progression_rate: Increase in velocity of 0.3 m/s, increase in pressure gradient of 7 mm Hg, decrease in valve area of 0.1 cm².

aortic_sclerosis.definition: Focal areas of valve calcification and leaflet thickening with an aortic velocity <2.0 m/s.
aortic_sclerosis.risk: Progression to severe AS occurs in about 10% of patients within 5 years.

cardiac_catheterization.purpose: For determination of severity of AS when noninvasive testing is non-diagnostic or there's a discrepancy.
cardiac_catheterization.calculation_methods: Fick or thermodilution cardiac output measurement.

recommendations.recommendation: Exercise testing in asymptomatic patients with severe AS (Stage C1) is reasonable.
Exercise testing should not be performed in symptomatic patients with severe AS (Stage D1).

exercise_testing.benefits: Provides diagnostic and prognostic information in asymptomatic severe AS.
exercise_testing.risks: Should not be done with severe symptoms or hemodynamic compromise.

General Information: Recording aortic valve hemodynamics with exercise is challenging and not typically beneficial for clinical outcomes.
Exercise testing risk is low for asymptomatic patients with AS but high for symptomatic ones.
Medical therapy for aortic stenosis (AS) includes managing hypertension and hyperlipidemia using GDMT.
ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy may reduce mortality in patients with AS post-TAVI.
Hypertension is common in patients with AS and may increase cardiovascular risk and mortality rate.
Recommendations: Patients at risk of developing AS should have hypertension managed according to GDMT and monitored.
Statin therapy is recommended for patients with calcific AS for prevention of atherosclerosis.
In patients post-TAVI, ACE inhibitors or ARBs may reduce mortality.
Statin therapy not recommended for prevention of AS progression.
Additional Notes: No new beneficial effects of antihypertensive medications on cardiac output in AS confirmed by studies.
Consideration for specific antihypertensive medication effects on LV and prevention measures for CAD in AS patients.

content.1: In adults with severe high-gradient AS (Stage D1) and symptoms of exertional dyspnea, HF, angina, syncope, or presyncope by history or on exercise testing, AVR is indicated.
content.2: In asymptomatic patients with severe AS and an LVEF <50% (Stage C2), AVR is indicated.
content.3: In asymptomatic patients with severe AS (Stage C1) undergoing cardiac surgery for other indications, AVR is indicated.
content.4: In symptomatic patients with low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with reduced LVEF (Stage D2), AVR is recommended.
content.5: In symptomatic patients with low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with normal LVEF (Stage D3), AVR is recommended if AS is the most likely cause of symptoms.
content.6: In apparently asymptomatic patients with severe AS (Stage C1) and low surgical risk, AVR is reasonable when exercise test shows decreased exercise tolerance or a fall in systolic blood pressure of ≥10 mm Hg.
content.7: In asymptomatic patients with very severe AS (velocity of ≥5 m/s) and low surgical risk, AVR is reasonable.
content.8: In apparently asymptomatic patients with severe AS (Stage C1) and low surgical risk, AVR is reasonable when BNP level is >3 times normal.
content.9: In asymptomatic patients with high-gradient severe AS (Stage C1) and low surgical risk, AVR is reasonable when serial testing shows increase in aortic velocity ≥0.3 m/s per year.
content.10: In asymptomatic patients with severe high-gradient AS (Stage C1) and a progressive decrease in LVEF on at least 3 serial imaging studies to <60%, AVR may be considered.
content.11: In patients with moderate AS (Stage B) undergoing cardiac surgery for other indications, AVR may be considered.

content.Summary: The recommendation-specific supportive text discusses the management of high-gradient aortic stenosis (AS), particularly for symptomatic patients. It emphasizes the timing of interventions such as aortic valve replacement (AVR) and transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI). Key points include the importance of timely surgical intervention, outcomes related to surgical and transcatheter procedures, and considerations for asymptomatic patients with severe AS.
content.Interventions: For symptomatic patients with severe AS, AVR should be considered. Outcomes after AVR generally show symptom improvement and enhanced exercise tolerance. For asymptomatic patients, careful monitoring is required.
content.Clinical Considerations: The guideline describes considerations such as left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), aortic valve area, and low-flow, low-gradient AS in patient assessment.
content.Risk Assessment: Prospective clinical studies suggest disease progression in asymptomatic patients, supporting the value of timely monitoring and possible intervention.

content.guideline_title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.focus: Management of aortic stenosis (AS) through timing of interventions such as surgical AVR (SAVR) or transcatheter AVR (TAVR).
key_points: AS stages are categorized to guide intervention: Severe AS Stage D1, Stage D2, Stage D3, AS Stage C, and AS Stage B.
Interventions are based on criteria such as peak aortic jet velocity (Vmax), mean systolic pressure gradient, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), and exercise tolerance.
AVR is recommended for symptomatic severe AS and asymptomatic severe AS with reduced LVEF or abnormal response to exercise.
Periodic monitoring is emphasized for asymptomatic and symptomatic patients not meeting intervention criteria.
Risk factors for considering elective AVR in asymptomatic patients include rate of symptom onset and increased cardiovascular risk.
Exercise testing is useful for assessing symptoms in severe AS and identifying cases for potential intervention.

clinical_guideline_summary: Surveillance has shown that early surgery benefits patients with aortic velocity ≥4.5 m/s.
In asymptomatic adults with severe AS, elevated BNP levels are linked to a higher risk of AS-related events.
Hemodynamic progression in AS can lead to symptom onset, often once aortic velocity reaches ≥2 m/s.
Rapid disease progression predictors include older age and severe valve calcification.
AS patients require periodic monitoring for symptom development and progressive disease.
Low surgical risk suggests elective AVR may be beneficial for symptom onset prevention.

recommendations.Description: In patients with an indication for AVR, the choice of prosthetic valve should consider the patient's values, preferences, and related risks.
Bioprosthetic AVR is recommended for patients of any age requiring AVR who cannot manage VKA anticoagulant therapy.

content.shared_decision: The choice of prosthetic valve type is influenced by patient-specific factors such as age, expected durability, risk of anticoagulation, and preferences. Mechanical valves may be considered for younger patients, while bioprosthetic valves may be more appropriate for those who cannot maintain long-term anticoagulation therapy.
content.anticoagulation_therapy: VKA therapy is necessary for patients with mechanical valves to prevent thrombosis. Newer agents have not been established as safe for these patients.

age_based_recommendations.patients_under_50: Mechanical valves are recommended due to higher bioprosthetic deterioration risk.
age_based_recommendations.patients_50_to_65: Choice between mechanical or bioprosthetic valves should be individualized.
age_based_recommendations.patients_over_65: Bioprosthetic valves are recommended over mechanical valves.

content.pregnancy: Anticoagulation considerations are important for women desiring pregnancy.
content.treatment_options: The Ross procedure is mentioned as an option for some patients with appropriate anatomy.

clinical_guidelines.details: Newer surgical bioprosthetic valves have less structural deterioration in older individuals, but mechanical valves may still offer a survival advantage due to low bleeding and thromboembolism risk. Decisions should consider age, durability, bleeding, and reoperation rates.

recommendations.recommendation: A bioprosthetic valve is reasonable, particularly with high structural integrity and life expectancy considerations.

recommendations.candidates: Younger patients with specific anatomical and tissue characteristics where anticoagulation is risky.

decision_criteria.procedure: Transfemoral TAVI preferred if no contraindication, after evaluating life expectancy and durability.

decision_criteria.age_group: >80 years or younger patients with life_expectancy <10 years

decision_criteria.conditions: Severe AS, LVEF <50%, age ≤80, no contraindication to TAVI
decision_criteria.recommendation: Follow symptomatic patient recommendations for SAVR vs TAVI.

decision_criteria.conditions: Severe AS, abnormal exercise test, or elevated BNP

recommendations.description: For patients with an indication for AVR for whom a bioprosthetic valve is preferred but valve or vascular anatomy or other factors are not suitable for transfemoral TAVI, SAVR is recommended.

For symptomatic patients of any age with severe AS and a high or prohibitive surgical risk, TAVI is recommended if predicted post-TAVI survival is more than 12 months with an acceptable quality of life.

For symptomatic patients with severe AS for whom predicted post-TAVI or post-SAVR survival is less than 12 months or for whom minimal improvement in quality of life is expected, palliative care is recommended after shared decision-making, including discussion of patient preferences and values.
In critically ill patients with severe AS, percutaneous aortic balloon dilation may be considered as a bridge to SAVR or TAVI.

synopsis.decision_making: Patients choosing between SAVR and TAVI should consider surgical risk and expected valve durability. SAVR has over 50 years of usage data. TAVI generally lasts around 5 years. A key decision factor is the patient’s life expectancy.
synopsis.patient_age: In the U.S., average life expectancy is stated as ages 25 at 60 years, 17 at 70 years, and 10 at 80 years for women. For men, expected additional years are 22 at age 60, 14 at age 70, and 8 at age 80.
synopsis.research_coverage: Clinical trials mainly cover older patients with higher surgical risk. Little data exist for patients under 65.

recommendation_specific_text.SAVR: Offers excellent durability for mechanical and bioprosthetic valves. Studies with older patients show mean age in the mid-80s.
recommendation_specific_text.TAVI: Less durability in younger patients who face intermediate surgical risk. Recommendations for patients over 65 focus on shared decision-making.

content.title: Choice of SAVR Versus TAVI When AVR is Indicated for Valvular AS
content.subject: Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.guidelines: The flowchart outlines decision-making for aortic valve replacement (AVR) in patients with aortic stenosis (AS). It includes various factors such as patient age, risk assessment, and life expectancy. The decision is influenced by whether the risk is high or prohibitive and if vascular anatomy is suitable for transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI). The options are between surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) with a mechanical or bioprosthetic valve and TAVI based on these assessments.

content.1: SAVR is associated with a lower risk of paravalvular leak, less need for pacemaker, and lower reintervention rates compared to TAVI.
content.2: TAVI is a safe and effective procedure for severe symptomatic aortic stenosis (AS) with better outcomes on mortality and stroke risk but involves higher rates of certain complications.
content.3: AS patients with LVEF <50% have specific indications for AVR.
content.4: Published RCTs suggest careful consideration in choosing between TAVI and SAVR based on patient values and clinical conditions.
content.5: TAVI is evaluated primarily for transfemoral vascular access route with lower mortality but considerations on comorbidity.

content.Table Title: A Simplified Framework With Examples of Factors Favoring SAVR, TAVI, or Palliation Instead of Aortic Valve Intervention

Age/life expectancy.Favors TAVI: Older age/fewer expected remaining years of life

Valve Anatomy.Favors TAVI: Calcific AS of a trileaflet valve

Favors SAVR: Mechanical or surgical bioprosthetic valve preferred
Favors TAVI: Favorable ratio of life expectancy to valve durability
Severe calcification of the ascending aorta
Previous cardiac surgery with at-risk coronary grafts
Favors Palliation: Severe MR attributable to annular calcification

Favors TAVI: Severe lung, liver, or renal disease
Favors Palliation: Symptoms likely attributable to noncardiac conditions
Moderate to severe involvement of ≥2 other organ systems

Frailty.Favors SAVR: Not frail or few frailty measures
Frailty.Favors TAVI: Frailty likely to improve after TAVI
Frailty.Favors Palliation: Severe frailty unlikely to improve after TAVI

Favors SAVR: Valve anatomy, annular size, or low coronary ostial height precludes TAVI
Vascular access does not allow transfemoral TAVI
Favors TAVI: Previous cardiac surgery with at-risk coronary grafts
Favors Palliation: Valve anatomy, annular size, or coronary ostial height precludes TAVI
Vascular access does not allow transfemoral TAVI

Favors SAVR: Improved long-term exercise capacity and QOL
Accepts longer hospital stay, pain in recovery
Favors TAVI: Accepts uncertainty about valve durability and possible repeat intervention
Prefers shorter hospital stay, less postprocedural pain
Favors Palliation: Life prolongation not an important goal
Avoid futile or unnecessary diagnostic or therapeutic procedures

content.TAVI: The choice of Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) versus palliative care is based on a decision-making process considering patient values and preferences. It includes discussion of indication, risks, and benefits. The survival benefit is seen only in selected patients with conditions like advanced age, frailty, pulmonary disease, etc. TAVI is not recommended for patients with a life expectancy of less than one year or survival with benefit less than 25% of two years.
content.AS: Aortic stenosis patients may benefit from percutaneous aortic balloon dilation, although this is generally used temporarily. It's not a substitute for Aortic Valve Replacement (AVR), used more as a bridge to TAVI or SAVR in some severe cases.
content.AR: Aortic Regurgitation (AR) is detailed with the necessity for urgent diagnosis and intervention being lifesaving. TTE or TEE is indispensable in confirming Acute AR, CT imaging for acute aortic dissection, and MRI is rarely used.
content.Acute AR Intervention: In cases of AR from infective endocarditis or aortic dissection, medical therapy or beta blockers might be used to stabilize heart rate and reduce LV afterload, but use cautiously, especially if severe AR is due to conditions like acute aortic dissection.

content.Title: Stages of Chronic AR

Valve Anatomy: Diseases of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta
History of rheumatic fever or known rheumatic heart disease
Mild to moderate calcification of a trileaflet valve BAV (or other congenital valve anomaly)
Hemodynamic Consequences: Normal LV volume or mild LV dilation

Valve Anatomy: Dilated aortic sinuses or ascending aorta
IE with abnormal leaflet closure or perforation
Asymptomatic Severe AR.Additional Requirements: Diagnosis of chronic severe AR requires evidence of LV dilation
Hemodynamic Consequences: CI: Normal LVEF (>55%) and mild to moderate LV dilation (LVESD <50 mm)
C2: Abnormal LV systolic function with depressed LV LVEF (≤55%) or severe LV dilation (LVESD >50 mm or indexed LVESD >25 mm/m²)
Asymptomatic Severe AR.Symptoms: None; exercise testing is reasonable to confirm symptom status

Valve Anatomy: Dilated aortic sinuses or ascending aorta
Previous IE with abnormal leaflet closure or perforation
Symptomatic Severe AR.Additional Requirements: Diagnosis of chronic severe AR requires evidence of LV dilation
Hemodynamic Consequences: Symptomatic severe AR may occur with normal LV systolic function (LVEF >55%), mild to moderate LV dysfunction (LVEF 40% to 55%), or severe LV dysfunction (LVEF <40%)
Moderate to severe LV dilation is present
Symptomatic Severe AR.Symptoms: Exertional dyspnea or angina or more severe HF symptoms

content.Guideline: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.Focus: Diagnosis and management of chronic Aortic Regurgitation (AR)

Diagnostic Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with signs or symptoms of AR, TTE is indicated for assessment of the cause and severity of regurgitation, LV size and systolic function, prognosis, and timing of valve intervention.
In patients with a BAV or with known dilation of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta, TTE is indicated to evaluate the presence and severity of AR.

In patients with moderate or severe AR and suboptimal TTE images or a discrepancy between clinical and TTE findings, TEE, CMR, or cardiac catheterization is indicated for assessment of LV systolic function, systolic and diastolic volumes, aortic size, and AR severity.

Medical Therapy Recommendations.Recommendation: In asymptomatic patients with chronic AR (Stages B and C), treatment of hypertension (systolic blood pressure >140 mm Hg) is recommended.

In patients with severe AR who have symptoms and/or LV systolic dysfunction (Stages C2 and D) but a prohibitive surgical risk, GDMT for reduced LVEF with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and/or sacubitril/valsartan is recommended.

content.Synopsis: There is no evidence that vasodilating drugs reduce severity of AR or alter the disease course in patients with significant AR in the absence of systemic hypertension. Recommendations for GDMT for hypertension and HF apply to patients with chronic asymptomatic AR, as for the general population. In symptomatic patients who are candidates for surgery, medical therapy is not a substitute for AVR. Medical therapy is helpful for alleviating symptoms in patients with high surgical risk due to comorbid conditions.
content.Timing of Intervention: Recommendations for timing vary based on the severity of AR and left ventricular function. Interventions are suggested for symptomatic patients and certain asymptomatic patients based on specific LV function metrics and overall health considerations.

Recommendations.Details: In symptomatic patients with severe AR (Stage D), aortic valve surgery is indicated regardless of LV systolic function.
In asymptomatic patients with chronic severe AR and LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF ≤55%) (Stage C2), aortic valve surgery is indicated if no other cause for systolic dysfunction is identified.
In patients with severe AR (Stage C or D) undergoing cardiac surgery for other indications, aortic valve surgery is indicated.
In asymptomatic patients with severe AR and normal LV systolic function (LVEF >55%), aortic valve surgery is reasonable when the LV is severely enlarged (LVESD >50 mm or indexed LVESD >25 mm/m2).
In patients with moderate AR (Stage B) undergoing cardiac or aortic surgery for other indications, aortic valve surgery is reasonable.

In asymptomatic patients with severe AR and normal LV systolic function at rest (LVEF >55%; Stage C1) and low surgical risk, aortic valve surgery may be considered with evidence of worsening LV function or dilation.
In patients with isolated severe AR with SAVR indications, TAVI should not be performed.

content.Supportive Text: Severe AR is linked to a wide pulse pressure. Vasodilating drugs generally do not reduce blood pressure effectively without influencing heart rate too. Surgery, particularly valve replacement, is crucial for managing chronic severe AR.

content.Title: Timing of Intervention for Aortic Regurgitation

Intervention Recommendations.Condition: Asymptomatic (Stage C) with LVEF >55% and LVESD >50 mm
Asymptomatic (Stage C) with Progressive Decrease in LVEF to <55%-60% or Increase in LVEDD to >65 mm

General Information: AVR (Aortic Valve Replacement) is crucial for chronic severe AR (Aortic Regurgitation) patients manifesting symptoms, as it enhances survival and functional status.
LVEF (Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction) is a significant determinant of success post-AVR; surgery should be timed before LVEF falls below 55%.
LVESD (Left Ventricular End-Systolic Dimension) is a critical measure for surgical decisions in LV systolic dysfunction.
Alternative surgical options are explored for patients with moderate AR and other cardiac surgery needs.
LVEDD (Left Ventricular End-Diastolic Dimension) is an important marker for identifying at-risk asymptomatic patients.
TAVI (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation) is considered for specific severe AR patients but has limitations.
Considerations: For AVRs, symptom severity and LVEF reduction are critical.
LVESD needs careful indexing, especially in certain demographics.
Assessment involves a variety of diagnostic methods including TTE, TEE, and CMR.
Risks of TAVI must account for anatomical challenges.

content.Title: Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV) and Associated Aortopathy
content.Synopsis: BAV is a congenital anomaly affecting 0.5% to 2.0% of adults, more common in males. It can lead to a variety of valve diseases and aortic aneurysms. Patients require ongoing evaluation of the valve and aorta throughout life.

Diagnostic Recommendations.Description: TTE is recommended for patients with BAV to evaluate valve morphology, AS and AR severity, and aortic dimensions. It also checks for aortic coarctation.
CMR or CT angiography is suggested when TTE cannot assess aortic morphology accurately.
TTE screening in first-degree relatives of BAV patients is advised for detecting BAV or asymptomatic aortic dilation.

Clinical Insights: BAV is associated with aortic dilation and dissection risks.
Regular imaging and evaluation are necessary due to potential aortopathy.
Specific genetic cause for BAV is unknown; inheritance patterns vary.

content.Guideline: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.Focus: Intervals for imaging the aorta in patients with a Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV)

Key Points.Detail: Perform TTE (transthoracic echocardiography) including measurement of aortic sinuses and ascending aorta, or consider CMR (cardiac magnetic resonance) or CTA (computed tomographic angiography) if TTE is not adequate for aortic measurements.

Key Points.Criteria: If Aortic diameter (sinuses or ascending aorta) ≥ 4.0 cm
Key Points.Follow-up: Periodic imaging by TTE, CMR, or CTA.
Key Points.Interval Determination: Based on degree and rate of progression of aortic dilation and family history of aortic dissection (2a).

Key Points.Patients: BAV with prior aortic valve replacement
Key Points.Recommendation: Continued lifelong periodic imaging if aortic diameter is ≥ 4.0 cm (2a).

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with BAV and a diameter of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta of ≥ 4.0 cm, lifelong serial evaluation of the size and morphology is reasonable. Examination intervals should be determined by the degree and rate of progression of aortic dilation and by family history.
In patients with BAV who have undergone AVR, continued lifelong serial interval imaging of the aorta is reasonable if the diameter of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta is ≥ 4.0 cm.

content.Synopsis: Patients with BAV (Bicuspid Aortic Valve) often require lifelong surveillance due to the progression of valve disease and the growth of the aorta. Diagnostic imaging plays an integral role in monitoring patients.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Aortopathy is present in 20% to 40% of patients with BAV and is linked with dilation of the aortic sinuses, ascending aorta, and/or arch.
In patients with BAV who have indications for SAVR and a diameter of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta >4.5 cm, replacement is recommended.
Patients with high-risk factors like rapid diameter change or family history of aortic dissection may need more frequent monitoring.
Studies suggest that the aorta can continue to dilate in patients with BAV post valve replacement surgery.

Replacement of the Aorta: Operative intervention is recommended for patients with a BAV and aortic diameter >5.5 cm.
For a diameter of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta of 5.0 to 5.5 cm, surgery is reasonable at a Comprehensive Valve Center.
For low-risk patients or those who meet criteria for replacement, valve-sparing surgery may be considered.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
Clinical Guidance: Intervention for replacement of the aorta in patients with a Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV) is recommended based on the aortic sinus or ascending aortic diameter.
Surgical intervention is suggested for patients with a BAV and aortic diameter of the aortic sinuses or ascending aorta ≥5.5 cm.
Family history of aortic dissection, aortic growth rate >0.5 cm/year, or aortic coarctation are risk factors for aortic dissection.
Intervention is also reasonable at diameters of 5.0-5.5 cm with risk factors or when a conservative approach meets specific conditions at a Comprehensive Valve Center.
Specific indications include aortic sinus or ascending aortic diameter ≥4.5 cm when undergoing AVR due to severe AS or AR.
Valve-sparing surgery may be considered at a Comprehensive Valve Center.

content.Synopsis: The guidelines address timing for aortic valve intervention in patients with BAV and AS or AR, emphasizing that the procedures are similar to those for trileaflet aortic valves. The choice of prosthetic valve type is also similar, though unique BAV considerations apply. Surgical aortic valve repair is feasible in selected patients based on anatomy and tissue characteristics; such surgery should be performed at a Comprehensive Valve Center.

Recommendation.2b C-LD: Patients with BAV and severe AR meeting criteria for AVR may consider aortic valve repair at a Comprehensive Valve Center.
Recommendation.2b B-NR: Patients with BAV and symptomatic, severe AS may consider TAVI instead of SAVR with detailed consideration of risks and preferences at a Comprehensive Valve Center.

Additional Information.AVR Decision Factors: The decision to operate considers risks including aortic dissection, and weighing surgery against future dissection risks. Patients with BAV and diameters of aortic sinuses or ascending aorta between 5.0 and 5.5 cm should consider surgery if low risk.
Additional Information.Trial Data: Recent trials highlight TAVI benefits in symptomatic AS, although excluded young BAV patients showed higher paravalvular leak rates. Stroke rates are higher in BAV groups.
Additional Information.Mitral Stenosis: Rheumatic MS incidence is high in certain regions and more common in women. Variations in presentation depend on regional disease prevalence.

content.Stages of MS: Stages are defined by patient symptoms, valve anatomy, valve hemodynamics, and the consequences of valve obstruction on the left atrium (LA) and pulmonary circulation. Rheumatic valve disease is the primary cause, with anatomical features reflecting this disease process.
content.Hemodynamic Severity: Best characterized by valve area, either directly planimetered by 2D or 3D imaging or calculated from the diastolic pressure half-time. Severe MS is indicated by a mitral valve area ≤1.5 cm².
content.Diagnosis and Follow-Up of Rheumatic MS: Recommendations include the use of TTE for diagnosis in patients with signs or symptoms of rheumatic MS and TEE for patients considered for percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy to assess the presence or absence of LA thrombus and evaluate the severity of MR.

content.Synopsis: For patients with rheumatic MS, TTE is the initial diagnostic test to determine the severity of the stenosis and suitability for PBMC. If PBMC is being considered, a TEE can further evaluate the presence and severity of concomitant MR and rule out LA thrombus.
content.Imaging: TTE is the imaging modality of choice for rheumatic MS. 3D echocardiography provides better accuracy. Doppler echocardiography should report mean transvalvular gradients. Estimated RV systolic pressure is obtained from the TR velocity.
content.Evaluation: Concomitant MR should be quantified. Several scores evaluate mitral valve morphology and prediction of outcomes with PBMC. Assessment includes valve thickening, mobility, and calcification. Additional assessment of LA and LV compliance should be considered if needed.
content.TEE and MR: TEE offers better visualization as an alternative approach to assess rheumatic MS in patients whose TTE images are technically limited. TEE is useful for evaluation of MR severity.
content.Changing Signs or Symptoms: Patients with rheumatic MS may experience a change in symptoms due to increased hemodynamic load or shifts in the peri-operative period.
content.Routine Follow-Up: Rheumatic MS is a slowly progressive disease with a latent phase. Regular TTE at intervals dictated by valve area is important even without symptoms.
content.Cardiac Catheterization: Useful in patients where there is discordance between clinical and echocardiographic findings. It further characterizes hemodynamics and etiology of symptoms. Gorlin equation can be used for an independent calculation of mitral valve area.

content.Title: Recommendation for Diagnostic Testing: Exercise Testing in Patients with Rheumatic MS

Recommendations.Text: In patients with rheumatic MS and a discrepancy between resting echocardiographic findings and clinical symptoms, exercise testing with Doppler or invasive hemodynamic assessment is recommended to evaluate symptomatic response, exercise capacity, and the response of the mean mitral gradient and pulmonary artery pressure.
In patients with rheumatic MS and 1) AF, 2) a prior embolic event, or 3) an LA thrombus, anticoagulation with a VKA is indicated.
In patients with rheumatic MS and AF with a rapid ventricular response, heart rate control can be beneficial.
In patients with rheumatic MS in normal sinus rhythm with symptomatic resting or exertional sinus tachycardia, heart rate control can be beneficial to manage symptoms.

content.Synopsis: Exercise testing with hemodynamics is important if resting hemodynamics do not match clinical symptoms in patients with rheumatic MS. Anticoagulation decreases thromboembolic events in patients with rheumatic MS and AF, especially with prior embolic events or when an LA thrombus is identified. Heart rate control with beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, or ivabradine is advised in patients with tachycardia.

general_information: Patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis (MS) have increased risks of developing atrial fibrillation (AF) and embolic events due to changes in the left atrium (LA), such as enlargement and fibrosis.
Non-vitamin K oral anticoagulation has not been studied in patients with rheumatic MS.
Rate and rhythm control in AF is more challenging in rheumatic MS patients due to hemodynamic changes.
Medications like beta blockers or ivabradine can help relieve symptoms.

Recommendations for percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy (PMBC) are made for patients with severe MS, depending on valve morphology, presence of LA thrombus, and access to appropriate medical facilities.

content.Guideline: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.Synopsis: The optimal treatment of patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis (MS) is either percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy (PMBC) or surgery. These procedures require a high level of expertise and are recommended to be performed at experienced centers.
Recommendations: In symptomatic patients (NYHA Class II-IV) with rheumatic MS and specific adverse anatomy or risks, PMBC may be considered if performed at a Comprehensive Valve Center.
In severely symptomatic patients with mitral valve area less than or equal to 1.5 cm² and high surgical risk, PMBC may be considered.
The safety and efficacy of PMBC has been compared with surgical options in randomized trials.
Surgical commissurotomy remains an option for favorable valve morphology.
Mitral valve surgery is recommended in specific conditions such as major valvular thickening or fibrosis.
Pulmonary artery pressure assessments are important in management.
Clinical Factors: NYHA class, age, presence of arrhythmia (AF), are predictors of outcomes.
The natural history of rheumatic MS can be slow progression, but surgery should be delayed until there are limiting symptoms.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

Pathway.Condition: Stress test shows Hemodynamically Significant MS

content.Main Topic: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

Details: Calcific MS is due to calcification of the mitral annulus extending into the leaflets.
Leaflets remain usually unaffected.
Prognosis of calcific MS is poor with severe survival under 50%.
Interventions are different compared to rheumatic MS due to absence of commissural fusion.
Presence of severe calcification poses challenges for secure surgical intervention.
Recommendations: Valve intervention may be considered in severely symptomatic patients with extensive calcification.

Details: Acute MR may be caused by disruption in the mitral valve apparatus.
Spontaneous chordal rupture may occur with myxomatous mitral valve disease.

Stage A - At risk of MR.Valve Anatomy: Mild mitral valve prolapse with normal leaflet coaptation, Mild valve thickening and leaflet restriction
Stage A - At risk of MR.Valve Hemodynamics: No MR jet or small central jet area <20% LA on Doppler, Small vena contracta <0.3 cm

Stage B - Progressive MR.Valve Anatomy: Moderate to severe mitral valve prolapse with normal coaptation, Rheumatic valve changes with leaflet restriction and loss of central coaptation, Prior IE
Stage B - Progressive MR.Valve Hemodynamics: Central jet MR 20%-40% LA or late systolic eccentric jet MR, Vena contracta <0.7 cm, Regurgitant volume <60 mL, Regurgitant fraction <50%, ERO <0.4 cm², Angiographic grade 1+ to 2+
Stage B - Progressive MR.Hemodynamic Consequences: Mild LA enlargement, No LV enlargement, Normal pulmonary pressure

Stage C - Asymptomatic severe MR.Valve Anatomy: Severe mitral valve prolapse with loss of coaptation or flail leaflet, Rheumatic valve changes with leaflet restriction and loss of central coaptation, Prior IE, Thickening of leaflets with radiation heart disease
Stage C - Asymptomatic severe MR.Valve Hemodynamics: Central jet MR >40% LA or holosystolic eccentric jet MR, Vena contracta ≥0.7 cm, Regurgitant volume ≥60 mL, Regurgitant fraction ≥50%, ERO ≥0.4 cm², Angiographic grade 3+ to 4+
Stage C - Asymptomatic severe MR.Hemodynamic Consequences: Moderate or severe LA enlargement, LV enlargement, Pulmonary hypertension may be present at rest or with exercise

Stage D - Symptomatic severe MR.Valve Anatomy: Severe mitral valve prolapse with loss of coaptation or flail leaflet, Rheumatic valve changes with leaflet restriction and loss of central coaptation, Prior IE, Thickening of leaflets with radiation heart disease
Stage D - Symptomatic severe MR.Valve Hemodynamics: Central jet MR >40% LA or holosystolic eccentric jet MR, Vena contracta ≥0.7 cm, Regurgitant volume ≥60 mL, Regurgitant fraction ≥50%, ERO ≥0.4 cm², Angiographic grade 3+ to 4+
Stage D - Symptomatic severe MR.Hemodynamic Consequences: Moderate or severe LA enlargement, LV enlargement, Pulmonary hypertension present

Diagnosis of Acute MR.TTE: Initial imaging modality to evaluate LV function, RV function, pulmonary artery pressure, and mechanism of MR.
Diagnosis of Acute MR.TEE: Helps in detecting papillary muscle or chordal rupture or vegetations and annular abscesses.

Medical Therapy.Vasodilator Therapy: Improves hemodynamic compensation by reducing systemic vascular resistance.

content.context: The guideline discusses the assessment and management of primary (degenerative) and secondary (functional) mitral regurgitation (MR), focusing on the use of transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) and transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for diagnosis and management. It highlights the importance of determining the severity, etiology, and potential surgical interventions for primary MR, and emphasizes that volume overload in chronic MR can lead to myocardial damage and heart failure. The differentiation between etiologies, such as myxomatous degeneration and fibroelastic deficiency, is crucial for determining appropriate interventions.

content.General Information: The guideline discusses the evaluation and management of mitral regurgitation (MR), emphasizing the importance of valve repair over replacement when possible. Echocardiography techniques such as transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) and transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) are highlighted for their roles in assessing MR severity and in surgical planning. Accurate determination of MR severity is essential, with various parameters like regurgitant fraction, chamber volumes, and Doppler measurements contributing to the evaluation. The surgical approach, either repair or replacement, is determined based on these evaluations. TEE is particularly useful intraoperatively and in patients with primary MR who are at high surgical risk, aiding in the determination of repair feasibility and monitoring outcomes.

content.Synopsis: A repeat TTE provides clinically relevant information about patients who are being followed for primary MR who develop new-onset symptoms.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Description: The onset of symptoms in severe MR (dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, or declining exercise tolerance) is an indication for mitral intervention even if LV function is preserved. Symptoms are the culmination of the pathophysiology of MR and may indicate changes in LV or LA compliance; increase in pulmonary artery pressure; decrease in RV function; or the coexistence of TR. Therefore, symptoms add pathophysiological data not readily available from imaging.

Symptom onset is a crucial demarcation point in the natural history of MR and also a trigger for intervention. Because symptoms develop gradually, patients may fail to recognize or ignore symptoms. Natriuretic peptide levels provide objective evidence in patients with chronic severe MR.

Recommendations.Description: For asymptomatic patients with severe primary MR (Stages B and C1), TTE is indicated every 6 to 12 months for surveillance of LV function and assessment of pulmonary artery pressure.

In asymptomatic patients with severe primary MR (Stages B and C1), use of serum biomarkers and novel measurements of LV function, such as global longitudinal strain, may be considered as an adjunct to guide timing of intervention.

Indications: Inconclusive or discordant clinical assessments or noninvasive tests regarding MR severity, LV function, or need for surgery.
Evaluation of symptomatic status and hemodynamics in cases with possible cardiac cause of dyspnea.
Diagnostic Testing.Noninvasive Imaging: Often adequate for evaluation of MR.

Diagnostic Testing.Recommendations: Reasonable for patients with primary MR (Stages B and C) and symptoms attributable to MR. Exercise echocardiography or hemodynamic exercise testing is recommended.
Diagnostic Testing.Purpose: Establish true exercise tolerance, provide baseline for future assessments, display any possible abnormal pathophysiology during exercise.

Medical Therapy.Condition: Primary MR with LV Systolic Dysfunction (Stages C2 and D)
Medical Therapy.Recommendation: Guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for systolic dysfunction is reasonable if surgery is not possible or must be delayed.

Medical Therapy.Condition: Primary MR with Normal LV Systolic Function (Stages B and C1)
Medical Therapy.Recommendation: Vasodilator therapy not indicated if patient is normotensive.

content.Summary: The guideline addresses management of patients with primary mitral regurgitation (MR), focusing on treatment strategies and recommendations for surgical intervention.
Patient Management: In patients with MR and left ventricular (LV) dysfunction, the standard treatment for heart failure (HF) includes beta-adrenergic blockade, ACE inhibitors or ARBs, and possibly aldosterone antagonists.
Beta blockers may improve surgical outcomes and delay LV dysfunction.
Vasodilator therapy has limited benefits in acute severe symptomatic MR.
Hypertension should be treated as it worsens MR severity.
Surgical Recommendations: Mitral valve intervention is recommended for symptomatic patients with severe primary MR (Stage D).
Mitral valve surgery is advised for asymptomatic patients with severe primary MR and LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF ≤60%, LVESD ≥40 mm).
Mitral valve repair is preferred when surgery is indicated and anatomy is favorable for repair.
Mitral valve repair is recommended for asymptomatic patients with severe primary MR and normal LV systolic function, ensuring a high likelihood of successful repair.
Surgery may be considered for asymptomatic patients with severe primary MR and normal LV function with progressive issue.
Edge-to-edge repair (TEER) is an option for severely symptomatic patients with high surgical risk.

content.Summary: The guideline focuses on the management of severe primary mitral regurgitation (MR) due to leaflet pathologies, with a strong emphasis on the importance of surgical repair. Mitral valve repair is preferred over replacement, especially in cases of rheumatic valve disease if durable. The timing of surgery is critical, ideally before left ventricular (LV) dysfunction develops. Repair success correlates with surgical volume, and centers with expertise are recommended. Mechanisms are outlined for when specific mitral surgeries should be performed. The guideline stresses early intervention to prevent adverse LV dysfunction.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Surgical repair should be prioritized for severe primary MR, especially in high-volume centers.
Mitral valve replacement to be avoided for patients with posterior leaflet limitation until repair is attempted.

Details: Repair of primary mitral valve disease is often complex but superior to replacement.
Timing of intervention is crucial to prevent irreversible LV dysfunction.
Surgical volume and expertise significantly impact outcomes.
Early intervention reduces the need for surveillance and risks associated with waiting.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

Stage D.Symptom: Symptoms due to MR, regardless of LV function

Condition: High or prohibitive surgical risk with anatomy favorable for transcatheter approach and life expectancy >1 year
Successful and durable repair possible for Degenerative MV disease
Successful and durable repair possible for Rheumatic MV disease

Interventions.Type: MV surgery at primary or CVC (1)
MV repair at primary or CVC (2a)

Condition.Surgical mortality risk: <1% with >95% likelihood of successful and durable repair without residual MR

Annotations.Notes: Color correspondence to Table 2, See Prosthetic Valve section (11.2)

Annotations.Extras: Patients with severe MR and LV dysfunction should be treated in a Comprehensive Valve Center. Surgical repair strategies should ensure durability to be effective and avoid complications.

Key Points: Severe MR may lead to worsening LV dilation and dysfunction.
Early MR repair strategy must provide durable repair to be effective.

content.Mitral Valve Repair: Mitral valve repair is preferred for treating mitral regurgitation (MR) when certain conditions are met, such as LV function being adequate for recovery post-repair. TEER is effective in reducing MR in severely symptomatic patients.
content.Rheumatic Mitral Valve Disease: Repair is less favorable in rheumatic mitral valve disease due to complex changes but may be suitable for certain low-risk patients.
content.Chronic Secondary MR: Associated with LV dysfunction. Therapy is debated and the condition is defined by specific regurgitant orifice criteria.

Stage A.Anatomy: Normal valve leaflets and annulus in patients with CAD or cardiomyopathy
Stage A.Valve Hemodynamics: No MR jet or a small central jet area <20% LA on Doppler; small vena contracta <0.30 cm
Stage A.Associated Cardiac Findings: Normal or mildly dilated LV size with regional wall motion abnormalities; primary myocardial disease with LV dilation and systolic dysfunction
Stage A.Symptoms: Symptoms attributable to coronary ischemia or HF may be present and respond to revascularization and appropriate medical therapy

Stage B.Anatomy: Regional wall motion abnormalities with mild tethering of mitral leaflet; annular dilation with mild loss of central coaptation of the mitral leaflets
Stage B.Valve Hemodynamics: ERO <0.40 cm²; regurgitant volume <60 mL; regurgitant fraction <50%
Stage B.Associated Cardiac Findings: Regional wall motion abnormalities with reduced LV systolic function; LV dilation and systolic dysfunction attributable to primary myocardial disease
Stage B.Symptoms: Symptoms attributable to coronary ischemia or HF may be present and respond to revascularization and appropriate medical therapy

Stage C.Anatomy: Regional wall motion abnormalities and/or LV dilation with severe tethering of mitral leaflet; annular dilation with severe loss of central coaptation of the mitral leaflets
Stage C.Valve Hemodynamics: ERO ≥0.40 cm²; regurgitant volume ≥60 mL; regurgitant fraction ≥50%
Stage C.Associated Cardiac Findings: Regional wall motion abnormalities with reduced LV systolic function; LV dilation and systolic dysfunction attributable to primary myocardial disease
Stage C.Symptoms: Symptoms attributable to coronary ischemia or HF may be present and respond to revascularization and appropriate medical therapy

Stage D.Anatomy: Regional wall motion abnormalities and/or LV dilation with severe tethering of mitral leaflet; annular dilation with severe loss of central coaptation of the mitral leaflets
Stage D.Valve Hemodynamics: ERO ≥0.40 cm²; regurgitant volume ≥60 mL; regurgitant fraction ≥50%
Stage D.Associated Cardiac Findings: Regional wall motion abnormalities with reduced LV systolic function; LV dilation and systolic dysfunction attributable to primary myocardial disease
Stage D.Symptoms: HF symptoms attributable to MR persist even after revascularization and optimization of medical therapy; decreased exercise tolerance; exertional dyspnea

Diagnosis Recommendations.Recommendation 1: For patients with chronic secondary MR (Stages B to D), TTE is useful to establish the etiology, assess regional and global LV remodeling and systolic dysfunction, severity of MR, and magnitude of pulmonary hypertension.

Diagnosis Recommendations.Recommendation 2: For patients with chronic secondary MR (Stages B to D), noninvasive imaging (stress nuclear/PET, CMR, or stress echocardiography), coronary CT angiography, or coronary arteriography is useful to establish etiology of MR and to assess myocardial viability.

Diagnosis Recommendations.Recommendation 3: For patients with chronic secondary MR with severe symptoms (Stage D) that are unresponsive to GDMT and are being considered for transcatheter mitral valve interventions, TEE is indicated to determine suitability for the procedure.

Diagnosis Recommendations.Recommendation 4: For patients with chronic secondary MR undergoing transcatheter mitral valve intervention, intra-procedural guidance with TEE is recommended.

content.Synopsis: In symptomatic patients with chronic secondary mitral regurgitation (MR), transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) is the initial diagnostic modality. Coronary anatomy and myocardial viability assessment can be helpful in management if ischemic MR is suspected. TEE guides interventional procedures.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: 1. TTE helps identify patients with primary MR and secondary MR. Chronic secondary MR of any severity has a worse prognosis. Initial TTE helps establish the cause and serves as a baseline for comparisons.
2. Prognosis is poor for ischemic and nonischemic MR, but ischemic MR allows for revascularization and LV function improvement. Long-term results show survival rate improvement.
3. Identified anatomic considerations detectable by TEE can determine patients for TEER. COAPT trial criteria exclude certain valve features.
4. TEE assists in clip positioning during mitral TEER.
5. Patients with severe secondary MR should receive GDMT for heart failure with reduced LVEF.
6. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, beta blockers, and aldosterone antagonists are recommended for MR management.
Recommendations for Medical Therapy: Patients with chronic severe secondary MR and HF with reduced LVEF should receive standard GDMT.
Engage a cardiologist expert in HF management for patients with chronic severe secondary MR and reduced LVEF.

content.guideline: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
key_points: Secondary MR often responds to guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT).
Mitral valve surgery or transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER) is recommended for specific patients with chronic severe secondary MR.
TEER is indicated for improving symptoms and prolonging life in patients with chronic severe secondary MR.
Surgery may be beneficial for those with mitral valve replacement or repair procedures.
Specific recommendations for intervention based on clinical symptoms and left ventricular function are provided.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with chronic severe secondary MR related to LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <50%) who have persistent symptoms (NYHA class III, IV) on GDMT, TEER is reasonable.
In patients with severe secondary MR (Stages C and D), mitral valve surgery is reasonable when CABG is undertaken for myocardial ischemia.
In patients with chronic severe secondary MR from atrial annular dilation with preserved LV systolic function (LVEF ≥50%), mitral valve surgery may be considered.
In patients with CAD and chronic severe secondary MR related to LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <50%), mitral valve surgery may be considered.
In patients undergoing mitral valve surgery because of severe symptoms with persistent GDMT for HF, mitral valve replacement may be reasonable.

clinical_trials: COAPT trial demonstrated improvement in survival for patients with secondary MR with persistent symptoms.
Mitra-FR trial compared MitraClip device with GDMT.

content.Title: ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.Subject: Secondary Mitral Regurgitation (MR) Management

Guideline Overview.Initial Step: Manage with guideline-directed management and therapy (GDMT) supervised by a heart failure (HF) specialist.

LVEF \u226550%.Additional Info: For severe persistent symptoms on optimal GDMT and atrial fibrillation (AF) treatment.

Mitral Anatomy Favorable.Criteria: LVEF 20%-50%, LVESD ≤70 mm, PASP ≤70 mm Hg

Mitral Anatomy Not Favorable.Additional Info: For persistent symptoms on optimal GDMT.

Additional Notes: Chordial-sparing MV replacement may be reasonable.
The effectiveness of surgical correction for chronic secondary MR to prolong life is uncertain.

Risks and Benefits.Consideration: Weigh in patients with LV systolic dysfunction.

Developments.Conditions: MR may develop with progressive LA dilation.

content.Valvular Heart Disease: The guideline discusses aspects of valvular heart disease concerning mitral and tricuspid valve diseases. It elaborates on issues like heart failure with preserved and reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), restrictive cardiomyopathy, and nonobstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
content.Mitral Valve Surgery: Surgery improves survival in symptomatic patients with secondary mitral regurgitation (MR) and improves quality of life. Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs) show mitral valve surgery reduces chamber size and improves oxygen consumption.
content.Tricuspid Valve Disease: Tricuspid Regurgitation (TR) is classified and staged, with significant TR often associated with poor long-term outcomes. Conditions include rheumatic disease, infective endocarditis, congenitally caused TR, and others like trauma and radiation.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

B.Valve Hemodynamics: Central jet ≤50% RA, Vena contracta width <0.7 cm, ERO <0.40 cm², Regurgitant volume <45 mL

C.Valve Hemodynamics: Central jet ≥50% RA, Vena contracta width ≥0.7 cm, ERO ≥0.40 cm², Regurgitant volume ≥45 mL, Dense continuous wave signal with triangular shape, Hepatic vein systolic flow reversal
C.Hemodynamic Consequences: Dilated RV and RA, Elevated RA with 'c-v' wave

D.Valve Hemodynamics: Central jet ≥50% RA, Vena contracta width ≥0.7 cm, ERO ≥0.40 cm², Regurgitant volume ≥45 mL, Dense continuous wave signal with triangular shape, Hepatic vein systolic flow reversal
D.Hemodynamic Consequences: Dilated RV and RA, Elevated RA with 'c-v' wave
D.Clinical Symptoms: Elevated venous pressure, Dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, ascites, edema

Recommendations for Diagnosis of TR.Recommendation: In patients with TR, TTE is indicated to evaluate the presence and severity of TR, determine the etiology, measure the sizes of the right-sided chambers and inferior vena cava, assess RV systolic function, estimate pulmonary artery systolic pressure, and characterize any associated left-sided heart disease.

In patients with TR, invasive measurement of the cardiac index, right-sided diastolic pressures, pulmonary artery pressures, and pulmonary vascular resistance, as well as right ventriculography, can be useful when clinical and noninvasive data are discordant or inadequate.

Synopsis: TTE can determine the etiology of TR and its effect on the RV.
Cardiac catheterization is of clinical value if the information from TTE is inadequate or discordant with the clinical presentation.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: TTE can distinguish primary TR (abnormal valve leaflets) from secondary TR (normal valve leaflets), define any associated left-sided valvular or myocardial disease, and provide an estimate of pulmonary artery systolic pressure.

Characterization of the severity of TR relies on an integrative assessment of multiple parameters, as recommended by the American Society of Echocardiography and European Association of Echocardiography, but many limitations remain.
Additional Information: In patients with TR undergoing left-sided valve surgery, annular diastolic diameter >40 mm indicates an increased risk of persistent or progressive TR after isolated mitral valve surgery.
Pulmonary artery systolic pressure is estimated from maximal TR velocity.
Assessment of RV systolic function is challenged by geometric and image acquisition constraints.

Normal RV systolic function is defined by several parameters, including tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion >16 mm, tricuspid valve systolic annular velocity >10.0 cm/s, and RV end-systolic area <20.0 cm² or fractional area change >35%.

content.guideline_title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.clinical_context: The guideline discusses the management of valvular heart disease, focusing on medical therapy for tricuspid regurgitation (TR) and timing of intervention.

recommendations: In patients with signs and symptoms of right-sided HF attributable to severe TR (Stages C and D), diuretics can be useful.
In patients with signs and symptoms of right-sided HF attributable to severe secondary TR (Stages C and D), therapies to treat the primary cause of HF, such as pulmonary vasodilators, can be useful.
therapies: Diuretic therapy treats systemic congestion in patients with severe symptomatic TR.
Low-salt diet and support stockings may help.
Aldosterone antagonists may be of additive benefit with targeted effects.
focus: Underlying etiology in secondary TR management.
Reduction of pulmonary artery pressures and vascular resistance.

recommendations.condition: In patients with severe TR (Stages C and D), tricuspid valve surgery is recommended.
In patients with progressive TR (Stage B), tricuspid valve surgery can be beneficial.
recommendations.context: In settings of tricuspid annular dilation or right-sided HF symptoms.

recommendations.condition: In patients with signs of right-sided HF and severe primary TR (Stage D), isolated tricuspid valve surgery can reduce symptoms and hospitalizations.

content.Synopsis: Treatment of secondary tricuspid regurgitation (TR) is targeted at pulmonary hypertension or myocardial disease. Surgical treatment is performed for selected patients with TR at the time of surgery for left-sided valve lesions to treat severe TR (Stages C and D) and to prevent later development of severe TR in patients with progressive TR (Stage B). Surgical intervention should be considered for isolated TR. Outcomes of patients with severe primary TR are poor with medical management, increasing interest in early surgery.

Recommendations.1: Severe TR of either primary or secondary etiology may improve after treatment of left-sided valve lesion and reduction of RV afterload; reoperation may be necessary if there is marked dilation of the annulus or intrinsic disease of the tricuspid leaflets.
Recommendations.2: If left untreated, secondary TR may progress, resulting in worse functional outcomes and survival. Risk factors include tricuspid annulus dilation and right ventricular dysfunction.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease

Treatment Recommendations.Condition: Severe TR with Right Heart Failure (Stage D)

Options.Situation: At time of left-sided valve surgery
Primary TR poorly responsive to GDMT
Secondary TR with annual dilation without PAP or left-sided disease
Prior left-sided valve surgery with absence of severe PH or RV systolic dysfunction
Primary TR with progressive RV dilation or systolic dysfunction
At time of left-sided valve surgery
Annual dilation >4.0 cm or prior right HF

content.TR Development: TR can develop in association with AF and annular dilation. Patients with AF-related TR have rapid progression of TR severity and chamber dilation.
content.Surgical Intervention for TR: Surgery improves quality of life in selected symptomatic patients with AF-related severe TR. Mortality rate for isolated TR in selected patients is acceptable (<4% to 5%).
content.Tricuspid Valve Surgery: Recommended for asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic patients with severe primary TR. Low surgical risk in healthy patients without comorbidities.
content.Reoperative Surgery Risks: High reoperative mortality rate for isolated tricuspid valve surgery is influenced by the advanced nature of RV failure and other complications.

content.Title: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Valvular Heart Disease
content.Focus: Management and intervention guidelines for patients with valvular heart diseases, specifically dealing with mixed aortic stenosis (AS) and aortic regurgitation (AR), and mixed aortic stenosis (AS) and mitral regurgitation (MR).

Key Recommendations.Recommendation: In symptomatic patients with a peak transvalvular jet velocity of ≥4.0 m/s or a mean transvalvular gradient of at least 40 mm Hg, AVR is recommended.
In asymptomatic patients with a jet velocity of ≥4.0 m/s with an LVEF <50%, SAVR is recommended.

content.Synopsis: The guideline discusses the similarities between moderate mixed disease and pure severe AS. It highlights the implications for valve replacement in patients with symptoms due to moderate mixed disease.
content.Imaging: TTE is recommended for assessing jet velocities and lesion severity.
content.Symptoms: Difficulty in symptom attribution due to the complexity of mixed valve disease; elevated BNP and filling pressures may indicate cardiac disease.
content.Management: Comprehensive imaging and clinical evaluation are necessary for decision-making in mixed valve disease.
